posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
May be a controversial opinion: overrated and overpriced are two different things. The former has to do with the hype around it, the other a value judgement on whether the price it commands is worth it or not.

Examples of both:

Overrated: you don’t think pinot noir is all that great of a grape despite many others loving it. You’re not a big fan of it at any price point.

Overpriced: You think DRC is great, but don’t think it’s worth $20k/bottle, if it was cheaper you’d buy it.

That said, those two concepts at times do go hand in hand, i.e. hype around a particular wine/regions tends to drive prices up.

I have too little experience with Burgundy to make any direct comments, but anything that becomes big and famous becomes overvalued. The more famous it is, the more ‘people who really don’t know anything about it’ start to become part of the demand and obviously as the demand goes up, the price goes up. The higher a percentage of the people buying it that fit into that category the less the price has to do with the quality. But even that is subjective.

For bourbon Pappy is the obvious. People who know nothing about bourbon are buying $4,000 bottles for celebrations because of the name. Pappy earned the respect by being one of the best bourbons out there, but those who know this drove the price to only about 30% of what it is today, the other 70% was driven by people who couldn’t tell the difference between pappy and Old Weller Antique. Being overrated or overpriced doesn’t mean its not very high quality.

People have been saying that Burgundy is overrated or overvalued for the 30 some odd years I’ve been in this hobby. Heck, I probably said it 25 years ago. However back then I had not tried much burgundy my palate had not tasted broadly and deeply enough to get to where it is today. I’m certainly not saying it has to be the best or anything for anyone. I am always in the let 1,000 flowers bloom camp in life. I know people who have been into wine for 30 years and would say the same thing. They are in the minority however for whatever reason based on my LT wino friends.

Personally I would agree Burgundy it is over valued, as are many things in life, per my own marginal utility. Overrated I would take issue with. Of the top 20 wines I have ever had the majority are from Burgundy but far from all. Maybe half are Burgundy. There are many fine wines from Burgundy well under $100 as there are from across the globe. I personally rarely spend the coin on Burgundy today other than a simple quaffer under $30. I’ll confess that if I want a daily chard fix I’d head to the Macon as I’d prefer that flavor profile at that price vs. most anything else. And personally I find very few new world pinots I like. So while I generally don’t agree with the substitution argument across disparate grapes, I do find Cru Bojo scrathes that itch reasonably well for me.

I agree that Burgundy is over rated. Yes, some of the wines are glorious, but the inconsistency is absolutely maddening. I have no idea how Burgundy can be so inconsistent and simultaneously so expensive. Charging thousands for White Burgs that have a 20% premox rate is crazy to me. In my experience, I’ve been at least mildly disappointed with half the Burgundy I’ve purchased. I’m not buying wines at the very top echelon, but to me, a truly great wine region should be able to produce consistently satisfying wine at all price points (excepting the very low end; everywhere produces cheap plonk after all). So, in my opinion, even though Burgundy produces some great wine, it is disqualified from being a truly great region, and the great wine it does produce, is insanely expensive. I think Burgundy is both overvalued and overrated. 9 out of 10 times I’d take a Champagne, Bordeaux, Barolo, or Alsatian riesling over Burgundy at any price point; $20, $50, $100, $500.

And just to add to the controversy, I fell the same way about Napa!

Only about 1% of all the Burgundies I have ever had I would consider astonishing, earthmoving experiences. When it is good, it is very, very good, but you can never predict what will come out of any given bottle. Most of these great Burgundies I have had were consumed in France and I really wonder if Burgundy simply does not travel well. Unlike Bordeaux, Burgundy was initially intended to be shipped out on barges to far closer destinations than where Bordeaux was going.

Does anyone share my experience that you have a higher chance of coming across a great bottle in France?

To confuse matters even more, a scatter plot of the great Burgundies I have had does not line up very well with the reputation/price axis. I have had many prestigious/expensive Burgundies (almost always in America) that were extremely disappointing.

I think Burgundy is possibly underrated, especially if you look at critics’ scores. On the other hand, most modern Bordeaux, CA Cabs, WA reds and other wines of this ilk are way overrated.

In the 1990s, much of the wine world started making more and more Parkerized wines. Burgundy tried this and mostly has rejected it. As Burgundy has returned to more traditional ways of making wine, as one new generation after another started improving farming and winemaking in the region (read some of the posts by William Kelley here about the innovative farming techniques) and as new wine critics like Allen Meadows, John Gilman and, now, William Kelley, started giving people more confidence in the Burgundies they were buying, the quantity of people buying Burgundy went way up and prices followed.

I like Philip’s idea;

I really wonder if Burgundy simply does not travel well

. Wine is always going to best at the domaine, but there is a fragility to Burgundy, especially red, that I can imagine being sensitive to heat and travel. Haven’ been to Burgundy myself, so I can’t say from first hand experience (unfortunately).

I did have a 1967 Remoissenet Chabolle-Musigny in the U.S. that was fantastic. Supposedly stored and reconditioned at the domaine prior to shipping in 2018, went from France, to Cali, then to me in CT. That wine travelled, and it did so as an old wine, but the majority of its life was spent under the streets of Beaune, so this is a bit of an unusual case.

We’ve all heard the stories of drinking amazing 70 or 80 year old wines Burgundies in domaine cellars, but I would never spend decent money on a Burgundy that old, figuring it must have turned to vinegar long ago. Maybe this travel theory has something to it!

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
It will be interesting to see what happens with Burgundy and global warming. Maybe they will be a tad bit riper and more like new world Pinots? Hence, more in line with the styles I like.

What Burgundy tastes like isn’t only due to its climate and terroir. In a very broad sense, winemaking styles and decisions common to the regions also make it taste “Burgundian.” Same can be said for wines that taste like the New World, it isn’t entirely due to climate, but also winemaking decisions.

That said, I’d imagine that climate change in long term has the potential affect Burgundy’s classification system in quite an impactful manner; declassifying certain GC and elevating some 1er cru to GC status. Burgundy is already seeing cooler areas like Saint Aubin, which struggled to ripen grapes every year now be able to ripen grapes consistently.

posts.name: Thomas Keim

Burgundy is dead at retail

It’s always been dead at retail. The best wines, value wines to rarities, rarely make it to an actual retail shelf. They are usually promised out (if not pre-paid for) months, if not years in advance. posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked:
I agree that Burgundy is over rated. Yes, some of the wines are glorious, but the inconsistency is absolutely maddening. I have no idea how Burgundy can be so inconsistent and simultaneously so expensive. Charging thousands for White Burgs that have a 20% premox rate is crazy to me. In my experience, I’ve been at least mildly disappointed with half the Burgundy I’ve purchased. I’m not buying wines at the very top echelon, but to me, a truly great wine region should be able to produce consistently satisfying wine at all price points (excepting the very low end; everywhere produces cheap plonk after all). So, in my opinion, even though Burgundy produces some great wine, it is disqualified from being a truly great region, and the great wine it does produce, is insanely expensive. I think Burgundy is both overvalued and overrated. 9 out of 10 times I’d take a Champagne, Bordeaux, Barolo, or Alsatian riesling over Burgundy at any price point; $20, $50, $100, $500.

And just to add to the controversy, I fell the same way about Napa!

You describe exactly why I, as a European, got interested in American imported wines in the 90’s. Because they were consistent. I still think that holds true today. You of course have quality differences and certain wines that don’t speak to you, but overall you can be pretty sure that if you spend $25+ on a CA/OR/WA wine, you’re going to get something drinkable. In Europe, there is no such guarantee from any region (with the worst offenders being France and Italy). They are wildly inconsistent and you can spend half a fortune on something that tastes exactly like the village cooperative table wine that costs €5 at the bodega.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
…but overall you can be pretty sure that if you spend $25+ on a CA/OR/WA wine, you’re going to get something drinkable. In Europe, there is no such guarantee from any region (with the worst offenders being France and Italy).

And I feel 100% the other way. I rarely open a West Coast red wine because of the monster sugar levels and so many of them are so overoaked and out of balance. I would put ANY $15 red from the Languedoc against any $25.00 bottle of West Coast red (especially the Rhone styled reds) and find a much more balanced, drinkable wine. I find 75% of the under $25 reds out of the West Coast to be damn near undrinkable.

The other issue is this; if you grew up drinking West Coast reds, and that’s all you drink today, your typical red from France or Italy is going to taste lighter, with MUCH less sugar extract. You probably find them innocuous with very little flavor.

yes, even as an American who cut his teeth long ago on some Cali pinots I had the same experience. A lot of California and Washington (even some Oregon) wines are so weirdly sweet compared to European wines that they almost feel like dessert wines on occasion. I find it quite off-putting. I wonder if it is terroir/climate or winemaking or both. There are certainly some winemakers who avoid this (like Halcon and Copain Syrahs) but it is so common, particularly for Pinot where even Copain can have trouble avoiding it, that it seems like there must be really strong pressure from climate etc.

Repeatedly experiencing this with American pinots does make me feel like Burgundy isn’t overrated, even if it is overpriced.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone

I don’t think sweetness is exactly the right word although I get where it comes from. Gloss, or polish, may be a better descriptor.

Perhaps. I think of it as more obvious/forward fruit.

The other issue is that (to my palate) many of the US wines tend to have much lower tannin, which makes the fruit level even more obvious. It’s why I try very hard to find US Pinots that have decent tannin structure.

I’ve often found people will conflate(sometimes purposely) sweet fruit flavors with residual sugar. In many new world Pinots there is often an absence of those sorts of secondary notes that distract or muddle the fruit into a more complex package. That allows that sweet fruit to stick out more as it’s the only thing to really pay attention to. Tannin and a more natural feeling acidity likely also contribute to this perception.

Plus of course there is often riper fruit though I think that difference is becoming less pronounced between here and there for more than one reason.

We’re never going to agree, as their are fundamental taste preferences, but the “sweet” term is too often misused IMO. The implication in both your posts is rampant, perceptible residual sugar in new world wines, and noble, absolute dryness, down to the last speck of sugar in Burgundy. It’s just not true at the general level you state it.

I am not a technical expert and I neither know nor does it matter that much to me as a consumer if the sweetness I perceive is due to residual sugar or something else. All I know is that I perceive it in many west coast wines, it is intense and one-note to the point of distracting from other elements of the wine, and it quickly becomes tiresome when you try to sit with a wine over dinner. If you are disagreeing with my perception, all I can say is it’s my perception - I guess I could consider whether it’s due to some unreasonable prejudice against west coast wines but I don’t think so. I don’t think it’s misusing the word “sweet” to use it to describe something that tastes sweet to you.

If you are disagreeing with a supposed explanation for this perception, you should reread my post to see that I did not offer any explanation for the perceived sweetness and did not ascribe it to residual sugar. You did that on your own and then proceeded to disagreed with it yourself as well

I am indeed curious as to the reasons for this perception and some of your explanations (lack of other structural and flavor elements of interest to distract from fruit sweetness) seem reasonable to me. It’s not like I never taste sweetness in Burgundy, I definitely do, it’s just that it’s not so oppressively one note or central to the experience of the wine. But then the question is, why do west coast wines have a harder time getting those other elements into the structure and flavor profile?

I agree with this in a general sense for cab wines made in US but there are some wineries that makes Old World wines.

Only about 1% of all the Burgundies I have ever had I would consider astonishing, earthmoving experiences. When it is good, it is very, very good, but you can never predict what will come out of any given bottle. Most of these great Burgundies I have had were consumed in France and I really wonder if Burgundy simply does not travel well. Unlike Bordeaux, Burgundy was initially intended to be shipped out on barges to far closer destinations than where Bordeaux was going.

Does anyone share my experience that you have a higher chance of coming across a great bottle in France?

To confuse matters even more, a scatter plot of the great Burgundies I have had does not line up very well with the reputation/price axis. I have had many prestigious/expensive Burgundies (almost always in America) that were extremely disappointing.

I’m sure other more experienced Burgundy drinkers will chime in, but I think there might be something to that. William Kelley touched on this in his Zoom AMA. He noted that one of the reasons/perks for moving to Burgundy was the opportunity to taste aged bottles that hadn’t traveled extensively.

I was hoping to expand on this. Maybe the “overrated” factor is due to the fact many of the bottles shipped out of France just don’t wind up as good.
In addition to the experience I described above,I also remember that when we buy the village Burgs in Burgundy restaurants, they are usually darn good.I usually don’t have such positive experiences with the village wines I taste here.

I wrote to three Burgundy wine making friends who come to the USA often and asked them if they see a difference in their wines in the USA as opposed to home. Two would not give a straight answer, possibly to avoid negative marketing, the third said the wines tasted here usually taste one year more evolved.

Would like to hear the experiences of other bi-hemispheric tasters.

With the above talk of West Coast reds and Burgundy, I have to say I have always enjoyed Burgundy, but could never afford the top level hooch. I have had some West Coast Pinot at the same time and found it strangely/palpably sweet in comparison, to the point where I couldn’t drink it and haven’t tried one for five years. I am sure they wouldn’t all be like that, but as Californian wine is generally expensive in the UK I would have to conclude that at the £50 range it is Burgundy that is not overrated.

yes, even as an American who cut his teeth long ago on some Cali pinots I had the same experience. A lot of California and Washington (even some Oregon) wines are so weirdly sweet compared to European wines that they almost feel like dessert wines on occasion. I find it quite off-putting. I wonder if it is terroir/climate or winemaking or both. There are certainly some winemakers who avoid this (like Halcon and Copain Syrahs) but it is so common, particularly for Pinot where even Copain can have trouble avoiding it, that it seems like there must be really strong pressure from climate etc.

Repeatedly experiencing this with American pinots does make me feel like Burgundy isn’t overrated, even if it is overpriced.

Modern farming is focused on creating the most efficient canopy possible. It’s my feeling that the monks in Burgundy didn’t worry about this as much. Irrigation exacerbates this by allowing the plants to grow bigger leaves as well. Most new world vineyards produce fruit with Brix upward of 23 and many reach 25+ Brix before fruit developes maturity. And many winemakers see hang time, and lower yields, as the path to success. Riper flavors, higher sugars, and lower acids.

In France, you lose your AOC if you irrigate.

In Champagne, the region that arguably achieves great flavor maturity at the lowest Brix, yields are roughly 2.5 times what many new world wineries feel is appropriate for crop load. Separating sugar accumulation(photosynthesis) from physiological ripening(time on the vine) is a hard jump for most growers.



So soil, farming, and attitude. There is a movement towards drier more savory wines in the states, but it’s a slow process.

They will end up supplanting much of my California Pinot spending, as I am finding I prefer some of the OR wines, and they are a better value.

Then we get to the terroir question, and I can say that I have found more obvious terroir distinctions in OR than in CA. That’s a very Burgundian drinking path, and the OR wines scratch that exploratory itch, while being delicious in their own right, and keeping me from going broke. I can drink interesting, site expressive Pinot Noir for a lower price than village Burgundies, and they also seem to mature a bit faster. As a person in my mid-50s I appreciate that as well.

Still wondering where those €15 quality village Burgs are to be found…

user_status.description: Wine doesn’t have to be tertiary to be enjoyable

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Question is why can’t the New World produce Riesling like the Germans, with few exceptions of course.

See Below:

I showed this to a viticulturist friend who asked if it was the Wehlener Monsantogarten… that said, neither of us were volunteering to cultivate slopes this steep by hand, without herbicides.

Another issue with New World Riesling, and especially US Riesling, is a lack of interesting vine genetics.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
I’m not necessarily agreeing with the whole train of thought here. However, what frustrates me quite a bit is when you taste at a winery, join a wine club, or even get an email deal from a winery…and then see their wine for cheaper on the store shelf (in person or online). Really blows my mind.

So, maybe my unpopular opinion is that wineries should definitively be the cheapest place to buy their wine… always. In other words…best deal should be ex-cellar.

I wouldn’t say that it’s “unpopular,” but it’s definitely unrealistic from a business perspective. If a winery wants distribution anywhere besides direct to consumer they need to price the wines accordingly for distributors and retailers to make money on the wines, too.

posts.name: Nate M
posts.cooked:
You don’t even drink Oregon Pinots lol.

Let me boil it down for the simple. I’ve tasted a bunch of $30-40 Oregon Pinots over the years and while I enjoyed many of them and many of them get high praise from others, they were on par with quality 15€ Village Burgs. Can you also buy 1er crus that are $90 and worse? Of course. But if a good number of quality producers in Burg sell their Village wines at 15€ or less (even though they are then marked up to $30-40 after being imported here), what makes your average OR/CA producer think his wine is worth $30-40 ex-cellar?

I’m not necessarily agreeing with the whole train of thought here. However, what frustrates me quite a bit is when you taste at a winery, join a wine club, or even get an email deal from a winery…and then see their wine for cheaper on the store shelf (in person or online). Really blows my mind.

So, maybe my unpopular opinion is that wineries should definitively be the cheapest place to buy their wine… always. In other words…best deal should be ex-cellar.

I sympathize with this thought and had the same reaction many years ago. Simple answer here. The three tier system is the only way to move wine to retail in most states.

Retailers, for the most part can not get ex cellars pricing.

Most wineries can not sell all of their wines ex cellars.

Retailers do not support wineries that undercut pricing.

Mostly, my answer is that we offer significant case discounts and have temporary release pricing for mailing list. So I can’t offer ex cellars pricing all the time or my main supporters get rightfully annoyed, but we do offer it.

That all sounds great. I think for me it feels almost insulting, on my part, to ask for a discount (or free shipping…or something) from the winery if I am buying more. However, I also have this thought in the back of my head that…”they must be making more from me than a retailer that buys from them”. If I am correct, it seems wineries should be likely to offer much better case discounts, incentives, or whatever to get the direct sale the best price even though it might not be “published”.

posts.cooked:
Simple math for the simple has it that 15 Euro is about $20 here.

If you come to my winery, you can get my Willamette Valley ex cellars for $25. With Berserker discount, it’s $20/bottle. Same-same as I see it.

All from dry farmed, old vine fruit, crafted low tech, and with a finished alcohol of 13.0%, no RS, a pH of 3.41, and 19 months in elevage before bottling. It may not be 1er Cru but it isn’t sweet.

I’m sure you’re expertise in Willamette Valley wines is far deeper than your posts make it seem.

Sounds like you are an exception to the rule. Great to hear! I’d love to find a Willamette Valley option that I like for $20. Do you ship to GA?

I agree that it is not feasible, but then I also think that wineries should be flexible with their pricing. Sometimes, it almost feels as if they are disincentivizing someone from becoming a club member, repeat customer, etc.

One example, that is obvious to anyone who looks is Ridge Geyserville. Currently, on the ridge website the Geyserville is $45 for a 750. posts.name: Philip Ente
posts.cooked:
I was hoping to expand on this. Maybe the “overrated” factor is due to the fact many of the bottles shipped out of France just don’t wind up as good.
In addition to the experience I described above,I also remember that when we buy the village Burgs in Burgundy restaurants, they are usually darn good.I usually don’t have such positive experiences with the village wines I taste here.

I wrote to three Burgundy wine making friends who come to the USA often and asked them if they see a difference in their wines in the USA as opposed to home. Two would not give a straight answer, possibly to avoid negative marketing, the third said the wines tasted here usually taste one year more evolved.

Would like to hear the experiences of other bi-hemispheric tasters.

I haven’t tasted my wines in the UK or Denmark as of yet, but I have tasted them between Oregon and the Washington DC area. There is an impact, and while they can still show very well on the east coast it’s definitely different.

I suspect at least part of that is bottle shock.

Also, if you store a wine for 10 years, that it moved once in its infancy is unlikely to make a huge difference. I expect it’ll make a much bigger difference for younger wines when any difference is magnified. Hopefully once you start drinking more of your aged wines on trips out east they’ll drink a lot more like the ones you’re having in Oregon

not a bottle shock issue. Many of these disappointing USA tasted premier cru Burgs have been stored by me 10 years or more.
Was more concerned about changes from the shipping here. Like I said, Burgundy was initially intended to be shipped by truck or barge to pretty nearby destinations, unlike Bordeaux.

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked:
I’m not necessarily agreeing with the whole train of thought here. However, what frustrates me quite a bit is when you taste at a winery, join a wine club, or even get an email deal from a winery…and then see their wine for cheaper on the store shelf (in person or online). Really blows my mind.

So, maybe my unpopular opinion is that wineries should definitively be the cheapest place to buy their wine… always. In other words…best deal should be ex-cellar.

I wouldn’t say that it’s “unpopular,” but it’s definitely unrealistic from a business perspective. If a winery wants distribution anywhere besides direct to consumer they need to price the wines accordingly for distributors and retailers to make money on the wines, too.

I agree that it is not feasible, but then I also think that wineries should be flexible with their pricing. Sometimes, it almost feels as if they are disincentivizing someone from becoming a club member, repeat customer, etc.

One example, that is obvious to anyone who looks is Ridge Geyserville. Currently, on the ridge website the Geyserville is $45 for a 750. At my local total wine the price if you are buying any mixed 6-pack is $34.19. To me, that is a steep difference. Even if ridge offers me a 20% case discount (I am a club member and that seems to be the best they offer) I am still better off buying it from total wine.

Anyways, it’s just an opinion of mine. I still love ridge and wine;)

It’s not I work at Ridge but I do have a winery and have not a completely different concept in place as far as direct and national sales (as far as I can tell). Membership in the various Ridge clubs does not include the Geyserville (it seems). Not that you COULDN’T buy it from them but I don’t think that’s what they’re going for. That is their national wine that drives sales with distributors. We have the Willamette Valley Reserve Pinot Noir. It’s never a part of our club offerings. You can buy it from us but you’re likely to find it locally (okay, locally might be a stretch it’s not like we’re everywhere) and maybe for less if you factor in every aspect of price (especially if we would have to ship it across the country). This is, IMO, a really good wine with petter pedigree than most Pinots at the $25-ish price point. It is there to keep our distributors engaged and give retailers a “go to” PGC wine for folks that aren’t spending more than say $30. We have no expectation that anyone will buy this wine directly from us. We don’t pour it (because people don’t buy it because they’re there for the 100-200 case crazy wines that aren’t available anywhere but the winery). If people contact us for this wine and we know it could be available to them locally (say, in Massachusetts) we tell them to go buy it there as we likely will be more expensive once we get done sending it FedEx. Folks in Oregon will buy it sometimes as an add in to the wines they’re picking up since they’re already there at the winery but it’s never a driver of sales in the DTC sense of the word for us. It has a whole other purpose. I’m sure that’s what Ridge does with the Geyserville bottling. You’re not supposed to order it from them if you live in Minnesota or wherever. They literally want you to buy it from your local retailer.

posts.name: Fred_Daner
posts.cooked:
Michael- my first thought was Oregon Pinot to keep the comparison with Pinot Noir but after looking at what I’ve consumed this year I’d say there are many 2016 Barolo Normale that are going to be a good bit better than the HN Bourgogne in the $35-40 range. I’m going to pick up both the 16 and 17 HN to do some blind tasting with friends. On another note around what makes up our cellars perhaps we work a few trades well stored classic Napa for Burgs

As for the original question that started this discussion my experience earlier this week with the 2008 Jadot CSJ is the reason I no longer buy Burgundy. It was very good but still very primary and not in the zone for me. It was pretty good on it’s own but did shine with the Short Ribs we were eating. The fat really softened and opened up the wine and overall we really liked it. I paid $90 for it back in 2014. Looks like the 16 and 18’s are now in the $170+ range which I would not pay. Following the definition of overrated I would say for me the answer would be yes. Many of the Village wines I used to buy are now in the $70+ range. I can buy Oregon Pinot and Barolo/Barbaresco in that range that need less cellar time and also more consistently deliver the goods for my palate.

Cheers



Finding value in Burgundy isn’t that hard. I can’t find a $30 red in the world that I’d prefer to 2017 Hudelot Noellat Bourgogne Rouge or a $30 white I’d prefer to 2017 PYCM Bourgogne Blanc.

I also believe that the berserker day deals might be the best deal anyone can get all year from many of you. Cheers!

In CA I can see this being a problem. It exists far less in Oregon. In general there aren’t really much in the way of other options in the immediate wine country vicinity from which to buy. Yes, there are grocery stores that have Oregon sets but largely they aren’t wines that one would be necessarily up against from a winery. There are only a handful of very, very small specialty shops in the area. I suppose it would be possible to find this or that at a place like New Seasons. It’s incumbent on a winery managing what wines go where and for what price at that point. Again, if people see the Reserve at a store for $26 as opposed to $29 or whatever we sell it for (I don’t really know off the top of my head because we don’t really sell it at the winery) that’s okay with me. I want people to buy it at that store. It’s great. It supports the network we work in and with. I can guarantee it would be impossible to find the vast, vast majority of our wines at all, let alone cheaper anywhere else. Places that are goofing with their customers by trying to do too many things with too few offerings will eventually hear it.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
But then the question is, why do west coast wines have a harder time getting those other elements into the structure and flavor profile?

Soil.

this, along with the even broader catch-all “terroir”, is everyone’s favorite brief explanation of everything in wine. But has there ever been scientific substantiation of how soil translates to wine flavors (beyond the obvious of water retention and drainage etc.)? It isn’t really believable that with the entire West Coast of the U.S. to play with you couldn’t find at least generally similar soil types to Burgundy.

If anyone (including any of our west coast winemakers) has any more detailed thoughts on this central mystery then I’m all ears…
